REASONS FOR DECISION
[1] XXXX XXXX (the "Appellant"), a citizen of Turkey, appeals a decision of the Refugee Protection Division (RPD) denying her claim for refugee protection. She has submitted new evidence in support of this appeal. The Appellant is requesting an oral hearing. The Appellant asks the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) to overturn the decision of the RPD and to find that the Appellant is a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection. In the alternative, the Appellant requests that the matter be returned to the RPD for a new hearing under a differently constituted panel.
Background
[2] The Appellant alleged before the RPD that she has a well-founded fear of persecution at the hands of the Turkish government due to the Appellant's ethnicity as a Kurd and her leftist political views. She also fears her abusive former fiancé who is now a policeman. Greater details may be found in the Appellant's Basis of Claim (BOC) form1 and in the audio recording of the hearing.
[3] The Appellant's application for refugee protection was heard on September 27, 2016. A negative decision was rendered in writing on October 18, 2016. The RPD found that the Appellant was unable to establish the core of her claim with trustworthy and credible evidence.
[4] Pursuant to section 111(1)(b) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA), the RAD sets aside the determination of the RPD and substitutes its determination that the Appellant is a Convention refugee.
Appellant's Issues
[5] The Appellant submits that the RPD erred:
a) When the RPD assessed the Appellant's state of mind; and
b) When the RPD gave little weight to the medical reports; and
c) When the RPD found that the Appellant's profile was insufficient to establish her claim.
Admissibility of New Evidence
[6] The Appellant has submitted new evidence in support of this appeal.
[7] The RAD must first determine whether the new evidence meets the requirements of section 110(4) of the IRPA. Could this evidence have been presented prior to the rendering of the RPD's decision and if not, why not? The fact that evidence presented does not go as anticipated is not a reason that other available evidence could not have been presented as well.
[8] The new evidence is being submitted in answer to issues brought forward at the RPD hearing wherein the panel had made findings based on the evidence adduced at the hearing, those findings were not favourable to the Appellant. The Appellant is, basically, providing the documents now which should have been before the RPD.
[9] The Federal Court stated in a recent case:
"I am satisfied that the RAD did not err in refusing to admit the applicant's proposed new evidence. The RAD's decision indicates that it was well aware of its role, that is that it assessed each piece of the proposed new evidence, including the applicant's submissions on the new evidence, in light of subsection 110(4) and, "within the context of the totality of the Appellant's evidence adduced at the RPD." The applicant's explanation for failing to place the proposed new evidence before the RPD was that he could not have foreseen that the RPD would make negative findings on: (1) his identity; and (2) the question of objective risk. This is simply not consistent with the record. I concur with the RAD's conclusion that the applicant had the onus to put forward his case to the RPD as to why he should be accepted as a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection. It was not open to him to wait to forward requisite and relevant evidence until the RPD rendered a negative determination."2
[10] Although a document may pass the requirements of section 110(4), it may not be automatically admitted as evidence. Once a document is deemed to have passed the test of section 110(4), it must be assessed for relevance, and credibility. It makes no sense to admit evidence if that evidence is not relevant or credible. I will consider each of the newly submitted documents as follows:
1) A statuary declaration with photographs explaining that one of her persecutors, her former fiancé is now a policeman. This evidence is submitted in accordance to Rule 29 and Rule 37.
2) An original psychiatric report from Turkey accompanied by additional details on the drugs prescribed to the Appellant.
3) Country condition articles which post-date the hearing.
[11] The first document is the declaration accompanied by photographs of the former fiancé in uniform, along with a photo of his badge and warrant card. The Appellant argues that this is new evidence because it was impossible to produce it pre-decision as the former fiancé only became a policeman after the decision of the RPD. I am concerned with this assertion. The statutory declaration by the Appellant dated XXXX XXXX, 20163 tells of her former fiancé becoming a policeman but does not say on what date he became a policeman. The declaration says that the Appellant was informed on XXXX XXXX XXXX 2016 that "my former fiancé is now a police officer." The decision was rendered on October 18, just XXXX days prior to the Appellant receiving her information. I find it implausible that a person could become a police officer in XXXX days, regardless of alleged family connections. Even though I find it less than credible that this former fiancé is so suddenly a police officer, I also find that if it is true, there would be no reasonable expectation for the Appellant to have known this earlier if he was not a police officer when she left Turkey. Although this is a stretch, I will allow this Rule 29 application for this document. It may be relevant and it appears to be credible.
[12] The psychiatric report from Turkey, however, is a different matter. The Appellant was represented at her hearing and should have adduced this document then. It was an obvious concern by the time of the hearing so it becomes more of an issue considering that there were three full weeks between the hearing and the decision, during which time, with a little effort, the Appellant could have retrieved the same document before the decision was rendered. For this reason, I find that this document is not new evidence as it fails the test of section 110(4) of the IRPA. It is not allowed into evidence.
[13] The Appellant has submitted a document which is an internet print-out on two drugs called XXXX and XXXX XXXX. These printouts appear to come from a reliable source (XXXX XXXX). They detail about everything one would need or want to know about each drug, including possible side-effects. Each has a long list of possible side-effects, with the list of side-effects as a clear warning for the consumer to contact his or her doctor "as soon as possible if any of the following side-effects occur". These are the side-effects which include hallucinations, mood changes, worsening depression (poor concentration) and many more. Some of these side-effects could definitely affect the ability of a person to testify coherently. The problem in this case is that there is insufficient medical evidence adduced to show that this Appellant suffered any of these side-effects. Had she suffered such side-effects, she should have immediately or as soon as possible, reported to a doctor. Had she done so, she could have obtained a note from the doctor verifying her issues. Without having done so and with no letter from a qualified doctor to confirm that she was even on medications during her hearing, this document becomes irrelevant. The Appellant argues that she offered to present the medicine bottles for each of the two drugs that she was prescribed. The problem with that offer is that her prescription was for "XXXX XXXX XXXX for 1 (one) month..." and that prescription was issued on XXXX XXXX 2016. Considering that the Appellant arrived in Canada in June, before her prescription could be renewed, it would be unlikely that by the time of the hearing on September 27, she would have any of the 30 day prescription left for her to be on. The Appellant has already testified that she had no doctor in Canada4 at the time of the hearing. I therefore find that this document is not of any probative value and as it is general information on drugs that are not new, then the document is not new in accordance to section 110(4) of the IRPA. This document is dismissed.
ANALYSIS
The Role of the RAD
[14] The RAD is an Appellate body governed by specific legislation5 and guided by the jurisprudence6 of the Federal Courts. The RAD's role and jurisdiction are best determined by looking at the legislative provisions creating the RAD and the appeal.7 Such an appeal "(i) is directed at the decision of the RPD; (ii) unless new evidence is accepted, is to be entertained on the basis of the record as it was constituted at the time of the RPD's decision; and (iii) is to be concerned solely with the errors of law, of fact or of mixed fact and law that, according to the appellant, the RPD made. This is the statutory configuration of an appeal before the RAD."
[15] The Federal Court of Appeal in its decision clearly states, that the RAD is to carefully consider the RPD's decision and then carry out its own analysis of the record to determine whether, as submitted by the Appellant, the RPD erred in its decision. The RAD is then to provide a final determination, either confirming the RPD's decision or setting it aside and substituting the RAD's own determination of the refugee claim. If the RAD cannot provide such a final determination without hearing the oral evidence already presented to the RPD, the matter can be referred back to the RPD.8
[16] When the RAD is dealing with the findings of fact and of mixed fact and law, the RAD is to review the RPD's decision applying a standard of correctness,9 with no deference accorded to the RPD.10
[17] The RAD will show deference to the RPD's findings on credibility where the RPD was in an advantageous position to reach such conclusions. The RPD's advantage comes from seeing claimants and witnesses and hearing oral testimony from those individuals.
[18] Where the RAD is to show deference to findings of the RPD, those findings must still be the result of an intelligible reasoning process. The RAD must be able to read the RPD's decision and comprehend how the RPD's conclusions were reached.
[19] Additionally, the outcome of that process must be a product that is based on and supported by the evidence that was before the RPD. It is for this reason, that it is principally important that the RAD employ its own review of the evidence in the RPD's record, in order to conclude whether the RPD's outcome is supported by that evidence.
[20] The RAD will apply a standard of correctness to its consideration of the RPD's decision: it will conduct its own review of the evidence and undertake its own analysis of the question. If this does not lead the RAD to the same conclusion as the RPD, the RPD's finding is wrong.
[21] However, the RAD will not interfere with the RPD's findings where such conclusions result from the RPD's distinct advantages or from an intelligible reasoning process whose premises are embedded in such advantages. If either the process or the conclusion does not meet these requirements, the RAD may determine the findings to be in error.
[22] Having done this, the RAD will provide a final determination, either by confirming the RPD's decision or setting it aside and substituting its own determination. If it cannot do so without hearing oral evidence that was already heard by the RPD, it will refer the matter back for redetermination.
[23] Accordingly, the RAD will conduct its own assessment of the RPD's decision and come to an independent assessment of whether the Appellant is a Convention refugee or a person in need of protection.
The Arguments
[24] The Appellant argues that the RPD erred in its assessment of the Appellant's state of mind. Counsel for the Appellant (counsel) argues that the RPD failed to take into account the evidence that the Appellant was medicated during her hearing and that the Appellant suffered from a mental disorder and symptoms.
[25] I disagree with counsel. The RPD was very clear in its Reasons and Decision11 that the panel did consider the question of medication and found that there was no documentation provided to support the claim of the Appellant being on medication at the hearing and had there been, there was no documentation to indicate that such medication may adversely affect the Appellant's ability to testify. Although the Appellant argues that at that time she had not seen a doctor in Canada, she was in fact represented by counsel who could certainly have arranged for the Appellant to see a medical doctor prior to her hearing. The Appellant argues that the panel had before it a document indicating that the prescription for XXXX was for one year; however, as noted above, the medical report from Turkey clearly indicates a 30 day prescription (which may be renewable) only. As the Appellant had not seen a Canadian doctor by the time of her hearing, there would be no legal means for her to have refilled that prescription. Therefore she would have been out of the medication long before her hearing. Without proof of a renewed prescription, it would be reasonable for the RPD to reject the theory of drug induced mental issues at the hearing,
[26] Without an original document from the psychiatrist, and in light of the credibility concerns of the panel, it was not unreasonable for the panel to put little weight on the copy of a document before it.
[27] In regards to the post-hearing document from XXXX XXXX, the report's author, Ms. XXXX, is a registered psychotherapist "specializing in the assessment and treatment of serious disorders of thought, cognition, mood, emotional regulation, perception, and memory." Her assessments are based on "in-depth clinical and psychodynamic interviews, typically extending for 60 minutes." 12
[28] While Ms. XXXX is not a medical doctor, psychiatrist, or psychologist, she appears to communicate a diagnosis: "...it is my clinical impression that Ms. XXXX is exhibiting symptoms consistent with post-traumatic stress disorder.... Ms. XXXX is also exhibiting symptoms consistent with generalized anxiety disorder and major depressive disorder."13
[29] Section 27 of the Regulated Health Professionals Act, S.O. 1991, Chapter 18 describes the communication of a diagnosis as a "controlled act," not to be performed unless the communicator is so authorized by a health profession Act, or unless the action has been delegated by a person who is so authorized. It does not appear that Ms. XXXX meets either qualification. The controlled act of diagnosis, at section 27(2)(1), is
Communicating to the individual or his personal representative a diagnosis identifying a disease or disorder as the cause of symptoms of the individual in circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that the individual or his personal representative will rely on the diagnosis.
[30] Ms. XXXX letter clearly sets out to communicate a diagnosis, to be relied upon in these proceedings. The RAD notes that contravention of the Regulated Health Professionals Act by a psychotherapist is considered professional misconduct.14 This assessment deserves little weight, as its author is neither a doctor nor a psychologist and therefore not qualified to make or communicate a diagnosis.15
[31] The RAD has also considered the manner in which the psychotherapist arrived at her conclusions. This process did not involve any tests or diagnostic tools. Rather, it appears that the Appellant participated in a one-hour interview, and the psychotherapist drew her conclusions on this basis. The RAD has no reason to believe that the report was written in the context of an on-going therapeutic relationship between the author and the Appellant.
[32] Further, Ms. XXXX went well beyond the scope of even a psychological or psychiatric report when she apparently made her own finding with respect to the Appellant's allegations of past persecution. She wrote, "I believe the information provided by Ms. XXXX was valid based on her emotional display and nonverbal behavior which were congruent with the themes presented in response to the questions and seemed to correspond with the experiences that Ms. XXXX has described. The fear, threats, physical violence and abuse that Ms. XXXX has described enduring in Turkey have clearly been deeply traumatic and it is evident that damaging and traumatic psychological after-effects persist."16
[33] It is the role of the RPD and the RAD, to determine the credibility of the Appellant's allegations and to determine the risk faced upon return to Turkey. Here the psychotherapist's report appears to have first strayed beyond her scope of practice - assessment and treatment17 - to provide a diagnosis, and then even further to conduct a credibility analysis and risk assessment. The author of the report has inappropriately mixed opinion with advocacy, and for this reason too her report warrants little, if any, weight.18
[34] Counsel argues that the panel erred by giving little weight to the medical reports disclosed at the hearing because these were not original documents. The panel failed to explain why an original document would be any different (in authenticity) from a copy. I concur with counsel that the panel did not explain why an original is of more probative value than a photocopy. However, this is not a fatal error and I will explain the differences for counsel.
[35] On original documents, changes which may be made to the document are very difficult to make so that they cannot be detected by the naked eye. As an example, one could "white out" a word and replace it with another. On an original, such a change would, on a balance of probabilities, be very visible to the naked eye. However, if one were to make that change carefully and then photocopy the document with the change on it, it becomes extremely difficult for an ordinary person, with the naked eye, to determine such a change. This is why an original document will most often be given much more evidentiary weight than a mere copy will be given. For this reason, I find that the RPD's assessment of these "copies" is a fair and correct assessment.
[36] In regards to the assessment of the psychiatric report's authenticity in light of XXXX report, I disagree with counsel. XXXX report has been given very little weight for the reasons above. The XXXX report has too many issues for it to overcome the problems with the other copies of medical reports.
[37] Counsel argues that regardless of all other issues, the Appellant's profile must be considered in the light of her being an Alevi Kurd who supported the leftist cause and the Kurdish and leftist coalition. Counsel further argues that the Appellant's political profile puts her at a high level of risk in Turkey especially when one considers the increased level of anti-Kurd, anti-Alevi and anti-opposition violence and other discriminatory acts by government forces.
[38] The panel has written on the Appellant's political profile and her profile as an Alevi Kurd. The panel noted that between 2013 when the Appellant was stopped by police at the XXXX XXXX rallies, and 2016 when she was arrested and held for a day, the Appellant had no issues with the police, even after the police (2013) had taken down all of the Appellant's personal data for their records. The Appellant had not set out any problems that she faced generally as an Alevi leftist, suggesting to me that her political and religious profiles were not high enough to be attracting any attention to the Appellant. That being said, however, I note that the panel also considered the objective evidence on hand, including the National Documentation Package (NDP) and those documents provided by counsel.
[39] The panel cites a number of discriminatory acts generally perpetrated against Alevi people, by the government, its agents and the general public. The panel continued to recognize that the situation in Turkey can be less than ideal for some Alevi. However, as noted by the panel, and confirmed in my independent review of the testimony and other evidence adduced, this well-educated, intelligent Appellant has not disclosed any example of either discrimination or persecution against her due to her faith as an Alevi. The 2013 incident at XXXX XXXX was nothing more than the police stopping anyone in the area of the protests and doing what the Metropolitan Toronto Police call "carding". The next incident, in XXXX of 2016, was a disgusting and disturbing abuse of power, if the Appellant's story is true. However, that abuse of power, based on the Appellant's own story, was nothing more than her former fiancé seeking revenge on her by reporting to the police that she was doing wrong and the police then abusing their power while abusing the Appellant.
[40] What I must consider, however, is the future for this Appellant. Refugee protection always requires a forward looking analysis. Regardless of what has or has not happened in the past, I must analyze what will, on a balance of probabilities, happen in the future. With this in mind, I have scanned the country condition documents disclosed by counsel as well as the NDP's Turkish information documents.
[41] Alevi people in Turkey are routinely denied their right to freedom of religion in a subtle manner by the state:
NDP item 12.2, 1.2 Religious Rights
Sources indicate that the Alevi are not recognized as a distinct religious group (Akdemir 2014, 65; Al Jazeera 4 Dec. 2014). Freedom House similarly states that the Alevi "lack protected status" (10 Mar. 2015). Country Reports 2013 states that "religious minorities, including Alevis ... are prohibited from fully exercising their linguistic, religious, and cultural rights and continued to face varying levels of pressure to assimilate" (US 27 Feb. 2014, 44). Sources indicate that mosques are being built in Alevi communities (Professor 21 May 2015; Reuters 2 Dec. 2014). Sources note that this is viewed by some as an attempt at assimilation (ibid.; Al-Monitor 11 Sept. 2013) or "conversion" (Professor 21 May 2015).
Sources indicate that the Turkish government does not recognize Alevi cemevis [houses of worship] as official houses of worship (ibid.; Al-Akhbar 9 Feb. 2015; EPC 24 Jan. 2014). Sources report that Alevi cemevis do not receive state funding (US 27 Feb. 2014, 45; Professor 21 May 2015; EPC 24 Jan. 2014), unlike Sunni mosques, which do (ibid.). Sources report that in December 2014, the European Court for Human Rights (ECHR) ruled against Turkey for failing to give the same status to Alevi houses of worship that mosques, churches and synagogues have (Professor 21 May 2015; Reuters 2 Dec. 2014; Today's Zaman 18 May 2015). Today's Zaman, a daily English-language newspaper in Turkey (ibid. n.d.), reports that in particular, the ECHR ruled in favour of the Alevi community in relation to electricity costs paid by individual cemevis, finding that the costs should be covered by the government's Religious Affairs Directorate and that the state's failure to pay for these costs amounts to discrimination (ibid., 18 May 2015). Al Jazeera reports that the Directorate pays for the electricity costs of mosques, churches and synagogues (Al Jazeera 4 Dec. 2014). Today's Zaman reports that the ECHR was to hold another hearing in June to further examine whether the fact that cemevis are not legally recognized by the state constitutes discrimination under the European Convention on Human Rights (18 May 2015). According to Hurriyet Daily News, a daily English-language newspaper in Turkey, the hearing was held on 3 June 2015 and the court is expected to have concluded its ruling within 6 to 12 months (3 June 2015).
Unlike Jewish and Christian students, Alevi students are unable to be exempted from compulsory [Sunni Islamic] religion classes (Norwegian Helsinki Committee Jan. Situation of Alevis, including political and religious rights; treatment of Alevis by society. 2015, 16; US 30 Apr. 2015, 187; RNS 18 Feb. 2015). According to sources, the ECHR ruled in September 2014 that the state's compulsory religious education for students discriminated against Alevis (Freedom House 2015; US 30 Apr. 2015, 187); and held that the state should allow pupils to be exempted from religious classes without parents having to disclose their religious beliefs (ibid.). According to a briefing produced by the German Information Centre on Asylum and Migration (IZAM), which provides specialist information to the Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF) in Germany (Germany 27 Feb. 2013), as of 23 February 2015, the Turkish government had "refused to implement" the ECHR ruling (ibid. 23 Feb. 2015).
[42] The Alevi people in Turkey are facing increased levels of discrimination by the state.
NDP item 12.2, 3. Treatment by Authorities
According to sources, since the civil war in Syria [2011], Alevi in Turkey have faced increased discrimination [2] (Professor 21 May 2015; Today's Zaman 22 Dec. 2013; Akdemir 2014, 69). In correspondence with the Research Directorate, a PhD candidate and sessional lecturer at the University of Sydney who has published multiple academic articles on the Alevi in Turkey (University of Sydney n.d.), stated that discrimination of Alevi has increased "since the 1980s, but even more so throughout the AKP government rule" (28 May 2015). The EPC article describes the Sunni-dominated governorship in the country as signalling an "institutionalized discrimination" [against Alevis] (24 Jan. 2014). Country Reports 2013 notes that Alevis "faced systemic discrimination from the state" (US 27 Feb. 2014, 45).
[43] According to these same documents, the Alevi have virtually no state protection in Turkey.
NDP item 12.2, 4.2 State Protection
According to the PhD candidate, there is no protection "specifically designed for Alevi victims" to their knowledge, and in reference to the marking of Alevi homes, "the fact that there has not been adequate investigation by state officials leaves the victims (and their Alevi witnesses) unprotected and in fear" (28 May 2015). In reference to the markings on apartments in an Alevi neighbourhood in Istanbul in October 2014, Today's Zaman quotes the CHP deputy as stating that "such incidents are not taken seriously by the government" and that "a police officer ... had implied that members of the Alevi community might have been behind the graffiti" (13 Oct. 2014).
Situation of Alevis, including political and religious rights; treatment of Alevis by society...
According to Human Rights Watch, referring to the incident in which an individual waiting in a cemevi courtyard was shot on 22 May 2014, "it took 26 hours before a prosecutor visited the scene" (Human Rights Watch 23 May 2014). In relation to the Gezi Park protests, the same source notes that "investigations and trials of the police for alleged killings during the Gezi protests in the summer of 2013 have proceeded slowly and have been highly flawed" (ibid.).
According to a report produced by the Congressional Research Service (CRS), a legislative branch agency within the US Library of Congress that provides policy and legal analysis to members of Congress (US n.d.), "[m]inority Muslim sects (most prominently, the Alevis) and non-Muslim religions largely depend on legal appeals, political advocacy, and support from Western countries to protect their rights in Turkey" (ibid. 1 Aug. 2014, 57). The Professor similarly stated that obtaining recourse through the courts "can be a possible solution, depending on the situation. However, in recent years the government has been weakening the independence of courts" (21 May 2015). Other sources indicate that as a result of government attempts to block an anti-corruption investigation in 2013, there is concern with political intervention in the judiciary and its impact upon the independence of the court system (Reuters 8 Oct. 2014; AI 24 Feb. 2014; Ozbudun Jan. 2015, 3). According to some sources, the intervention by the government resulted in the reassignment of police officers, prosecutors (US 27 Feb. 2014, 1-2; AI 24 Feb. 2014), and judges (ibid.).
[44] All of the foregoing tell me that, for Alevi Kurds in Turkey, state protection is neither effective nor adequate. The Turkish government fails to protect and worse still perpetrates much of the discrimination to which the Alevi are subject. Add to this, which on its own and in its totality may be sufficient discrimination to amount to persecution, I have a female Kurdish Alevi who alleges that she has been abused by a man who is now a member of the police department and that man may wish to cause harm to this Appellant due to past romantic issues.
[45] Turkey has an abysmal record of human rights abuses. These include, but are not limited to:
* Government interference with freedom of expression;
* Impunity and weak administration of justice and inconsistent application of the law;
* Inadequate protection of civilians;
* Arbitrary or unlawful deprivation of life;
* Torture and Other Cruel, Inhuman, or Degrading Treatment or Punishment;
* Extremely poor and inadequate prison conditions;
* Arbitrary arrest and detention;
* Lack of fair and public trials.
[46] The question I must answer is "on a balance of probabilities, will this Appellant face persecution if she returns to Turkey?"
[47] In a fulsome and independent analysis of all of the facts at hand, while considering the relevant statutes, I find that I do have before me an Appellant who from a political perspective, based on the evidence adduced above and on a balance of probabilities, will face persecution as an Alevi; however, this Appellant is also likely to face persecution at the hands of her former fiancé. As I find that this Appellant would, on a balance of probabilities, face persecution in Turkey, I need only now consider whether she will have access to state protection should such persecution occur. I find that the Appellant would not have access to effective or adequate state protection in Turkey, based on the reasons above.
Disposition
[48] The RAD has found that the RPD decision cannot stand.
CONCLUSION
[49] Pursuant to section 111(1)(b) of the IRPA, the RAD sets aside the determination of the RPD and substitutes its determination that the Appellant is a Convention refugee.
(signed)
"David Lowe"
David Lowe
March 2, 2017
Date

15 See Boros v. Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2002 FCT 661, para. 13; Boros, Andras Erno v. M.C.I. (F.C.T.D., no. IMM-3915-00), Lemieux, June 12, 2002.
16 Exhibit P-2, Appellant's Record, page 30.
